* [River Tweed Fisheries Act 1830](/wiki/River_Tweed_Fisheries_Act_1830 "River Tweed Fisheries Act 1830")

([Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_language "Scottish Gaelic language"): *Uisge Thuaidh* /ɯʃgʲə huəj/), is a river 97 miles (156 km)[[1]](#cite_note-1) long that flows east across the [Border region](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border") in Scotland and northern England. [Tweed cloth](/wiki/Tweed_(cloth) "Tweed (cloth)") derives its name from its association with the River Tweed.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Tweed is one of the great [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") rivers of Britain and the only river in England where an [Environment Agency](/wiki/Environment_Agency "Environment Agency") rod licence is not required for [angling](/wiki/Angling "Angling"). The river generates a large income for the local borders region, attracting anglers from all around the world.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The **River Tweed**, or **Tweed Water** ([Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_language "Scottish Gaelic language"): *Uisge Thuaidh* /ɯʃgʲə huəj/), is a river 97 miles (156 km)[[1]](#cite_note-1) long that flows east across the [Border region](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border") in Scotland and northern England. [Tweed cloth](/wiki/Tweed_(cloth) "Tweed (cloth)") derives its name from its association with the River Tweed.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Tweed is one of the great [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") rivers of Britain and the only river in England where an [Environment Agency](/wiki/Environment_Agency "Environment Agency") rod licence is not required for [angling](/wiki/Angling "Angling"). The river generates a large income for the local borders region, attracting anglers from all around the world.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=River_Tweed&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*Tweed* may represent an [Old Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic") name meaning "border".[[4]](#cite_note-4) A doubtful proposal is that the name is derived from a non-Celtic form of the [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") root *\*teuha-* meaning "swell, grow powerful".[[5]](#cite_note-BLITON-5)

## Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=River_Tweed&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Course")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Tweed.tributaries.jpg/250px-Tweed.tributaries.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tweed.tributaries.jpg)

The catchment area of the Tweed

The River Tweed flows primarily through the scenic [Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders") region of Scotland. Eastwards from the settlements on opposing banks of [Birgham](/wiki/Birgham "Birgham") and [Carham](/wiki/Carham "Carham") it forms the historic boundary between Scotland and England.

It rises in the Lowther Hills at [Tweed's Well](/w/index.php?title=Tweed%27s_Well&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tweed's Well (page does not exist)") near the rising points of the [Clyde](/wiki/River_Clyde "River Clyde") -- draining northwest (10 kilometres (6.2 mi) from the Tweed's Well) -- and the [Annan](/wiki/River_Annan "River Annan"), draining south (1.9 kilometres (1.2 mi) from the Tweed's Well).

"Annan, Tweed, and Clyde rise oot o’ ae hillside" is a saying from the Border region.[[6]](#cite_note-6) East of [Kelso](/wiki/Kelso,_Scottish_Borders "Kelso, Scottish Borders"), it becomes a section of the eastern part of the border. Entering England, its lower reaches are in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), where it enters the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") at [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed"). For only 7 kilometres of its course is it purely in England, and for 30 kilometres it is between England and Scotland.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Catchment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=River_Tweed&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Catchment")]

The river east of [St Boswells](/wiki/St_Boswells "St Boswells") runs through a [drumlin field](/wiki/Drumlin_field "Drumlin field"). It is the relic of a paleo-[ice stream](/wiki/Ice_stream "Ice stream") that flowed through the area during the last glaciation. Major towns through which the Tweed flows include [Innerleithen](/wiki/Innerleithen "Innerleithen"), [Peebles](/wiki/Peebles "Peebles"), [Galashiels](/wiki/Galashiels "Galashiels"), [Melrose](/wiki/Melrose,_Scottish_Borders "Melrose, Scottish Borders"), [Kelso](/wiki/Kelso,_Scottish_Borders "Kelso, Scottish Borders"), [Coldstream](/wiki/Coldstream "Coldstream") and [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed"), where it flows into the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). Tweed [tributaries](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") include:

* [Whiteadder Water](/wiki/Whiteadder_Water "Whiteadder Water")
  + [Blackadder Water](/wiki/Blackadder_Water "Blackadder Water")
* [River Till](/wiki/River_Till,_Northumberland "River Till, Northumberland")
* [Eden Water](/wiki/Eden_Water "Eden Water")
* [Teviot Water](/wiki/River_Teviot "River Teviot")
* [Leader Water](/wiki/River_Leader "River Leader")
* [Ettrick Water](/wiki/River_Ettrick "River Ettrick")
  + [Yarrow Water](/wiki/Yarrow_Water "Yarrow Water")
  + [Tima Water](/w/index.php?title=Tima_Water&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tima Water (page does not exist)")
* [Gala Water](/wiki/Gala_Water "Gala Water")
* [Leithen Water](/wiki/Leithen_Water "Leithen Water")
* [Quair Water](/wiki/Quair_Water "Quair Water")
* [Eddleston Water](/wiki/Eddleston_Water "Eddleston Water")
* [Manor Water](/wiki/Manor_Water "Manor Water")
* [Lyne Water](/wiki/Lyne_Water "Lyne Water")
* [Holms Water](/wiki/Holms_Water "Holms Water")

The upper parts of the [catchment](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of the Tweed in Scotland form the area known as [Tweeddale](/wiki/Tweeddale "Tweeddale"), part of which is protected as the [Upper Tweeddale National Scenic Area](/wiki/Upper_Tweeddale_National_Scenic_Area "Upper Tweeddale National Scenic Area"), one of 40 [such areas](/wiki/National_scenic_area_(Scotland) "National scenic area (Scotland)") in Scotland which are defined so as to identify areas of exceptional scenery and to ensure its protection from inappropriate development.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Despite that the catchment straddles the border between Scotland and England, management of it – in terms of water quality, bio-security, and ultimately protection of the salmon of the River Tweed – is overseen by a single body, the River Tweed Commission.[[9]](#cite_note-16)

## River Tweed Trail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=River_Tweed&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: River Tweed Trail")]

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=River_Tweed&action=edit), work is in progress on the development of a 113-mile (182 km) walking and cycling route following the length of the river from [Moffat](/wiki/Moffat,_Dumfries_and_Galloway "Moffat, Dumfries and Galloway") to Berwick-on-Tweed. The work includes new sections of path, upgrades to existing paths, bridge replacement and repairs, pedestrian road crossings, lighting and signage. The path is expected to be completed during 2028.[[10]](#cite_note-17)
